next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

Marcus Webb signed the visitor log at 2:14 PM on a Thursday, printing his name in the same blocky capitals he'd used every week for fourteen months. The girl at the front desk — new, maybe twenty-two, acrylic nails clicking on her phone — didn't look up. He wrote *Lola Fernandez* in the resident column and *nephew* in the relationship column and clipped the visitor badge to his shirt pocket, the laminated edge warm from the previous visitor's body heat.

He knew this building the way a surgeon knows a body he's opened before. The turn past the activities room where three women sat in wheelchairs watching a cooking show at full volume, the host shouting about risotto while one woman slept and another picked at the vinyl armrest with her thumbnail. The smell shifting as he passed the kitchen — today it was some kind of fish, the institutional version where the breading was thicker than the fillet. The hand sanitizer dispensers every fifteen feet, most of them empty or jammed. He'd reported the empty dispensers twice when he worked here. The second time was part of the complaint.

Lola's room was 112A, shared with Betty Ostrowski, who had severe vascular dementia and spent her days facing the window. Betty's daughter had taped photographs to the wall beside her bed — grandchildren, a dog, a house with a flag out front — but Betty looked through them the way she looked through everything, her eyes tracking something the rest of them couldn't see.

"Mijo!" Lola said when he came in, and her face did the thing it always did, this warm crumpling that made her look ten...
open_hooks_to_carry: Harry drops the confused act completely — voice, posture, vocabulary all transform
Carmen's core argument: the medications are dispensed under her license, she faces prison
prev_tail_excerpt:
.... Most were sleeping. Mrs. Okafor was moaning. Mr. Petrelli was snoring through his open door with one foot hanging off the mattress, and she tucked it back under the sheet without waking him. In Room 112A, Lola Fernandez was curled on her side with one hand under her cheek and the other wrapped around her gold crucifix, and she looked like someone's grandmother, which she was.

In Room 114A, Harry Zielinski was on his back with the blanket pulled to his chin and his eyes closed. No towel in sight. He looked like a frail old man dreaming about his dead wife, and Carmen stood in his doorway and watched his chest rise and fall and knew he was awake and knew he knew she was there and knew the performance was still the only thing either of them could afford to do in the dark.

She finished her rounds and went back to the sub-station. She had seven hours left on her shift. She charted, answered two call bells, helped Devon turn a patient at 5 AM, and drank the coffee she'd brought in a thermos that was now cold and tasted like the inside of the thermos. The folded count sheet stayed in her pocket. At shift change she would count the cabinet again with the day nurse. Then the forty-seven pills would exist in somebody else's numbers besides hers.

The hallway stayed empty. Dawn came slowly through the window at the end of Wing A, first gray, then pink, then the flat white light of a September morning that made the linoleum look like what it was. By the time she left, the count would have another witness. Then she'd know whether the next step belonged to the supervisor or to Harry.

setups_to_plant.description: Harry hints at why the ring started — references the pharmacy switch and medication delays without giving the full story; the complete version is withheld for later

state_deltas_required: Carmen's legal liability (meds under her name) versus the human cost of reporting elderly residents
Carmen enters a negotiation she didn't intend; by giving Harry a week, she reveals she's already more compromised than she admits
